   136→
   137→## 5. Data Flow: Session Lifecycle
   138→
   139→### 5.1 Session Creation (Spawn)
   140→
   141→```
   142→Developer -> POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/assign { taskId }
   143→  -> Route Handler: validate workspace access
   144→  -> Assignment Guard: validate task eligibility
   145→  -> [Local provider only] Worktree Manager: create git worktree
   146→  -> Session Lifecycle:
   147→       1. Create agent_session record in SurrealDB (status: "spawning")
   148→       2. Issue proxy token + DPoP credentials (existing flow)
   149→       3. Call sandbox_adapter.createSession({
   150→            agent: "claude",
   151→            cwd: worktreePath,
   152→            env: { ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL: proxyUrl, ... }
   153→          })
   154→       4. Configure MCP: sandbox_adapter.setMcpConfig("brain", {
   155→            type: "remote",
   156→            url: "/mcp/agent/<session-name>",
   157→            headers: { "X-Brain-Auth": agentToken }
   158→          })
   159→       5. Register persistence driver on SDK instance
   160→       6. Connect event stream -> event bridge -> SSE registry
   161→       7. Update agent_session: status "spawning"
   162→  -> Return { agentSessionId, streamId, streamUrl }
   163→```
   164→
   165→### 5.2 Multi-Turn Prompt
   166→
   167→```
   168→Developer -> POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/prompt { text }
   169→  -> Route Handler: validate workspace access
   170→  -> Session Lifecycle:
   171→       1. Lookup session in SurrealDB (not in-memory registry)
   172→       2. Validate status is promptable (spawning | active | idle)
   173→       3. Call sandbox_adapter.prompt(sessionId, [{ type: "text", text }])
   174→       4. Return 202 Accepted (prompt queued/delivered)
   175→       5. Agent processes prompt, events flow through event bridge
   176→```
   177→
   178→### 5.3 Session Restoration
   179→
   180→```
   181→[Network timeout or server restart detected]
   182→  -> SDK internally detects stale connection
   183→  -> SDK calls persistence_driver.listEvents(sessionId) to get history
   184→  -> SDK calls createSession() with fresh sandbox process
   185→  -> SDK rebinds session ID to new runtime ID
   186→  -> SDK replays recent events (max 50 events / 12,000 chars) as context
   187→  -> Event bridge reconnects to new event stream
   188→  -> SSE registry notifies client: "Session restored automatically"
   189→```
   190→
   191→### 5.4 Two-Plane Governance Flow
   192→
   193→```
   194→Coding Agent in Sandbox:
   195→  |
   196→  |-- LLM API request --> Brain Proxy (/proxy/llm/anthropic)
   197→  |     -> X-Brain-Auth header for spend tracking
   198→  |     -> Forward to LLM provider with credentials
   199→  |     -> Record trace
   200→  |
   201→  |-- MCP tools/list --> Brain Dynamic MCP Endpoint (/mcp/agent/<name>)
   202→  |     -> Resolve effective toolset (can_use UNION possesses->skill_requires)
   203→  |     -> Return filtered tool list
   204→  |
   205→  |-- MCP tools/call --> Brain Dynamic MCP Endpoint (/mcp/agent/<name>)
   206→  |     -> Evaluate policy graph
   207→  |     -> Inject OAuth credentials from credential broker
   208→  |     -> Forward to upstream MCP server
   209→  |     -> Record trace with policy evaluation result
   210→  |     -> Return tool result
   211→  |
   212→  |-- Native tools (file, bash, git) --> Execute locally in sandbox
   213→       -> No governance (passthrough)
   214→       -> Permission handler may intercept dangerous operations
   215→```
   216→
   217→## 6. Integration Patterns
   218→
   219→### 6.1 SandboxAgent SDK Integration (HTTP/SSE)
   220→
   221→Brain communicates with SandboxAgent Server via HTTP API (REST + SSE). The SDK client library wraps these calls. Brain wraps the SDK behind an adapter interface (see component-boundaries.md).
   222→
   223→- **Protocol**: HTTP/SSE (not WebSocket)
   224→- **Authentication**: Session-scoped tokens
   225→- **Event delivery**: SSE from SandboxAgent Server, consumed by adapter, forwarded through event bridge
   226→- **Error handling**: Fail fast on connection errors; retry with backoff on transient failures
   227→
   228→### 6.2 Dynamic MCP Endpoint (MCP Protocol over HTTP)
   229→
   230→The coding agent inside the sandbox connects to Brain's `/mcp/agent/<name>` endpoint as a remote MCP server. This uses the standard MCP protocol (SSE transport) that Claude Code and other agents natively speak.
   231→
   232→- **Protocol**: MCP over SSE transport
   233→- **Authentication**: `X-Brain-Auth` header with per-session agent token
   234→- **Tool filtering**: `tools/list` returns only the agent's effective toolset
   235→- **Policy enforcement**: `tools/call` evaluates policy graph before forwarding
   236→- **Credential injection**: OAuth tokens injected by credential broker per tool call
   237→
   238→### 6.3 Session Persistence (SurrealDB)
   239→
   240→The persistence driver implements SandboxAgent's `SessionPersistDriver` interface, storing session records and events directly in the knowledge graph.
   241→
   242→- **Protocol**: SurrealDB SDK (WebSocket)
   243→- **Consistency**: Events persisted before acknowledgment (at-least-once)
   244→- **Buffering**: In-memory buffer during transient SurrealDB outages (< 30s)
   245→- **Batching**: 100ms write window for high-frequency event bursts
   246→
   247→## 7. External Integrations
   248→
   249→| Integration | Type | Contract Test Annotation |
   250→|------------|------|-------------------------|
   251→| SandboxAgent Server | HTTP/SSE API (localhost) | Contract tests recommended for SandboxAgent HTTP API -- consumer-driven contracts (e.g., Pact-JS) to detect breaking changes in 0.x SDK releases |
   252→| Upstream MCP Servers | MCP Protocol (various) | Already annotated in existing tool registry design |
   253→| LLM Providers | HTTP API (via proxy) | Already covered by existing proxy infrastructure |
   254→
   255→## 8. Architecture Enforcement
   256→
   257→| Rule | Enforcement Tool | Description |
   258→|------|-----------------|-------------|
   259→| Adapter interface boundary | dependency-cruiser | Orchestrator modules must not import SandboxAgent SDK directly; only through adapter |
   260→| No module-level mutable state | ESLint custom rule | Ban `let` declarations at module scope in orchestrator/ |
   261→| Dependency direction | dependency-cruiser | event-bridge, persistence-driver, permission-handler must not depend on session-lifecycle |
   262→| Port/adapter compliance | TypeScript strict types | Adapter interface defined as type; implementation injectable |
   263→
   264→## 9. Handoff Notes
   265→
   266→### For Platform Architect (DEVOPS wave)
   267→
   268→- **Development paradigm**: Functional (types-first, composition pipelines, pure core / effect shell)
   269→- **SandboxAgent Server deployment**: Rust binary must be available alongside Brain server. Local dev: direct binary. Production: containerized (Docker image from Rivet, or custom build).
   270→- **Port requirements**: SandboxAgent Server listens on port 4100 (configurable). Brain must reach it at `http://localhost:4100` (local) or via Docker network.
   271→
   272→**External Integrations Requiring Contract Tests:**
   273→- SandboxAgent Server (HTTP/SSE API): session lifecycle, event streaming, permission handling. Recommended: consumer-driven contracts via Pact-JS in CI acceptance stage to detect breaking changes in 0.x releases before production.
   274→
   275→### For Acceptance Designer (DISTILL wave)
   276→
   277→- All acceptance criteria from DISCUSS wave user stories are preserved unchanged
   278→- New event types (`agent_permission_request`, `agent_permission_response`, `agent_restoration`) need SSE stream assertions in acceptance tests
   279→- Session persistence requires SurrealDB assertions (`agent_session` sandbox fields and `sandbox_event` table)
   280→- Multi-turn prompt flow replaces the current 409 rejection -- this is the core behavioral change
   281→- Mock adapter injection point: `SandboxAgentAdapter` type in `sandbox-adapter.ts`
   282→

1→# Data Models: Sandbox Agent Integration
     2→
     3→## 1. Schema Extension to `agent_session`
     4→
     5→SandboxAgent session state lives directly on `agent_session`. Most fields already exist — only sandbox-specific fields are added.
     6→
     7→### Existing fields reused
     8→
     9→| SandboxAgent concept | Existing field | Notes |
    10→|---------------------|---------------|-------|
    11→| Runtime session ID | `external_session_id` | Already optional string. SandboxAgent runtime ID goes here (changes on restoration). |
    12→| Agent type | `agent` | Already required string. "claude", "codex", "opencode", etc. |
    13→| Status | `orchestrator_status` | Already has values like "running", "completed", "error". Extend with "restoring". |
    14→| Working directory | `worktree_path` | Already optional string. Worktree path for local, sandbox path for cloud. |
    15→| Workspace | `workspace` | Already required. |
    16→| Created | `created_at` | Already required. |
    17→| Started | `started_at` | Already required. |
    18→| Ended / destroyed | `ended_at` | Already optional datetime. |
    19→| Last event time | `last_event_at` | Already optional datetime. |
    20→| Error info | `error_message` | Already optional string. |
    21→| Worktree branch | `worktree_branch` | Already optional string. Still used for local provider. |
    22→
    23→### New fields
    24→
    25→```sql
    26→-- schema/migrations/NNNN_sandbox_agent_fields.surql
    27→BEGIN TRANSACTION;
    28→
    29→-- Sandbox provider: "local", "docker", "e2b", "daytona"
    30→-- Needed on restart restoration to determine isolation strategy
    31→-- (local = Brain manages worktree, cloud = sandbox handles isolation)
    32→DEFINE FIELD OVERWRITE provider ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    33→
    34→-- Session type discriminator: "claude_agent_sdk" (current) or "sandbox_agent" (new)
    35→-- Needed to route session operations to the correct adapter on restart restoration
    36→DEFINE FIELD OVERWRITE session_type ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    37→
    38→COMMIT TRANSACTION;
    39→```
    40→
    41→### Fields NOT added
    42→
    43→| Considered field | Why excluded |
    44→|-----------------|-------------|
    45→| `mcp_endpoint_url` | Derivable from agent name: `${brainUrl}/mcp/agent/${agentName}` |
    46→| `event_count` | Derivable via query. Not on hot path. |
    47→| `restoration_count` | Observability metric — belongs on traces/spans, not session schema |
    48→| `last_restored_at` | Same — observability, not session state |
    49→| `replay_max_events` | SDK default (50) is sufficient. Override via adapter config, not per-session DB field. |
    50→| `replay_max_chars` | Same — SDK default (12,000) is sufficient. |
    51→| `last_event_seq` | Only needed for SurrealDB persistence driver (deferred). In-memory driver tracks this internally. |
    52→
    53→## 2. Session Event Persistence (Deferred)
    54→
    55→For R1 (local provider), the SDK's built-in `InMemorySessionPersistDriver` handles event storage for session restoration. Brain and the agent process share the same host — if Brain restarts, the local agent process dies too, so persisted events have no consumer.
    56→
    57→A custom SurrealDB `SessionPersistDriver` with a `sandbox_event` table and write buffering (ADR-077) is deferred until cloud provider support where sandboxes outlive Brain restarts. See GitHub issue for tracking.
    58→
    59→## 3. Permission Decision Storage
    60→
    61→Permission decisions are stored as trace records linked to the session, reusing the existing trace infrastructure rather than creating a new table.
    62→
    63→```sql
    64→-- Permission decisions use the existing trace table with specific fields:
    65→-- trace.type = "permission_decision"
    66→-- trace.metadata contains:
    67→--   permission_id: string
    68→--   tool_name: string
    69→--   arguments: string
    70→--   decision: "once" | "always" | "reject" | "timeout"
    71→--   decided_by: "user" | "auto_approve" | "timeout"
    72→--   response_time_ms: int
    73→```
    74→
    75→## 4. Migration from In-Memory Handle Registry
    76→
    77→### Current State
    78→
    79→```typescript
    80→// session-lifecycle.ts -- module-level mutable singleton (anti-pattern per AGENTS.md)
    81→const handleRegistry = new Map<string, AgentHandle>();
    82→```
    83→
    84→Session handles are stored in a `Map` at module scope. This violates the project convention against module-level mutable singletons and means all session state is lost on server restart.
    85→
    86→### Target State
    87→
    88→The `handleRegistry` is eliminated. Session lookup is always via SurrealDB:
    89→
    90→1. **Active session discovery**: `SELECT * FROM agent_session WHERE orchestrator_status IN ["running", "idle", "restoring"] AND workspace = $ws AND session_type = "sandbox_agent"`
    91→2. **Session restoration on restart**: Load active sandbox sessions and call `adapter.resumeSession(external_session_id)` for each
    92→3. **Prompt routing**: `sendSessionPrompt()` looks up the `agent_session` record, retrieves `external_session_id`, and calls `adapter.prompt()` — no in-memory handle needed because the adapter talks to SandboxAgent Server via HTTP
    93→
    94→### Migration Path
    95→
    96→The `handleRegistry` is removed in R1 as part of the session-lifecycle refactor. There is no data migration needed — the in-memory state was always ephemeral.
    97→
    98→## 5. Data Flow: Session Operations (R1)
    99→
   100→### Lifecycle transitions
   101→
   102→```
   103→createSession:
   104→  -> INSERT agent_session with session_type = "sandbox_agent", provider, orchestrator_status = "running"
   105→  -> adapter.createSession() → returns external_session_id
   106→  -> UPDATE agent_session SET external_session_id = $id
   107→
   108→prompt:
   109→  -> SELECT agent_session by RecordId
   110→  -> adapter.prompt(external_session_id, messages)
   111→  -> UPDATE agent_session SET last_event_at = time::now()
   112→
   113→destroy:
   114→  -> adapter.destroySession(external_session_id)
   115→  -> UPDATE agent_session SET orchestrator_status = "completed", ended_at = time::now()
   116→```
   117→
   118→### Event streaming (no persistence)
   119→
   120→```
   121→SandboxAgent emits UniversalEvent (session.onEvent())
   122→  -> Event bridge translates to Brain StreamEvent
   123→  -> SSE registry delivers to connected clients
   124→  -> No DB write (in-memory driver handles SDK-internal replay)
   125→```
   126→

1→# DESIGN Wave Decisions: Sandbox Agent Integration
     2→
     3→## Architecture Decisions
     4→
     5→### AD-01: Adapter Abstracts at SDK Level (Answers Q-04)
     6→
     7→**Decision**: The adapter interface wraps the SandboxAgent SDK instance (SDK level), not individual session handles (session level).
     8→
     9→**Rationale**: Session handles are SDK-specific objects with SDK-specific event emitter patterns. Wrapping at the SDK level means one adapter factory creates sessions that return Brain-owned `SessionHandle` types. The adapter boundary is a single point -- the factory function. Session-level abstraction would spread the SDK dependency across every session interaction.
    10→
    11→**Status**: Provisional
    12→
    13→---
    14→
    15→### AD-02: SurrealDB Replaces In-Memory Handle Registry
    16→
    17→**Decision**: Eliminate the module-level mutable `handleRegistry` Map. All session state is stored in SurrealDB (`agent_session` table with sandbox fields). Session operations (prompt, abort, etc.) look up the session record in SurrealDB and delegate to the SandboxAgent adapter via HTTP.
    18→
    19→**Rationale**: The `handleRegistry` violates the project convention against module-level mutable singletons (AGENTS.md). It also means all session state is lost on server restart, which directly conflicts with the restoration requirement (US-05). SurrealDB persistence is the source of truth; the adapter communicates with SandboxAgent Server via HTTP API, so no in-process handle is needed.
    20→
    21→**Status**: Provisional
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→### AD-03: Sandbox Fields on agent_session (Not a Separate Table)
    26→
    27→**Decision**: Add sandbox-specific fields (`provider`, `session_type`, `replay_max_events`, `replay_max_chars`, `last_event_seq`) directly to `agent_session` rather than creating a separate `sandbox_session` table.
    28→
    29→**Rationale**: Most SandboxAgent concepts already map to existing `agent_session` fields (`external_session_id`, `agent`, `orchestrator_status`, `worktree_path`, `ended_at`, `last_event_at`). Only 5 new optional fields are needed. A separate table would require joins on every session operation and transaction coordination on creation — unnecessary complexity for 5 fields. See ADR-076.
    30→
    31→**Status**: Provisional
    32→
    33→---
    34→
    35→### AD-04: 100ms Write Buffer for Event Persistence
    36→
    37→**Decision**: Buffer events in memory for 100ms before batch-inserting to SurrealDB. Per-session buffer with overflow protection.
    38→
    39→**Rationale**: SandboxAgent can emit 50+ events/second during test runs. Individual inserts at this rate would create excessive SurrealDB load. 100ms batching groups ~5 events per write at peak, reducing round-trips 5x while keeping latency well under the 500ms SLA.
    40→
    41→**Alternatives considered**:
    42→1. **Synchronous per-event writes** -- Simpler but creates backpressure at high event rates
    43→2. **1-second batch window** -- Lower DB load but risks 1s of event loss on crash
    44→3. **Background queue with separate writer** -- More complex architecture for marginal benefit
    45→
    46→**Status**: Provisional
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→### AD-05: Event Bridge Replacement with Forward-Compatible Unknown Event Handling
    51→
    52→**Decision**: Build a new event bridge (`sandbox-event-bridge.ts`) for SandboxAgent events. Unknown event types are logged at warn level and skipped (not crash). The persistence driver stores all events regardless of type (including unknown).
    53→
    54→**Rationale**: SandboxAgent SDK is 0.x and may add new event types in any release. The bridge must not break on unknown events. Storing raw events in `sandbox_event.payload` (FLEXIBLE object) ensures replay fidelity even for event types the bridge does not yet translate. This is the opposite of the fail-fast convention -- for event translation specifically, graceful degradation is the correct choice because a missing UI event is far less costly than a crashed session.
    55→
    56→**Status**: Provisional
    57→
    58→---
    59→
    60→### AD-06: Permission Handler as Separate Component
    61→
    62→**Decision**: Extract permission handling into a dedicated `permission-handler.ts` module, separate from the event bridge.
    63→
    64→**Rationale**: Permission requests are bidirectional (SandboxAgent -> Brain -> User -> Brain -> SandboxAgent), unlike other events which are unidirectional. The handler manages SSE delivery, timeout logic (60s auto-reject), auto-approve scope checking, and response routing. Mixing this with the event bridge would violate single responsibility.
    65→
    66→**Status**: Provisional
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→### AD-07: Session Restoration via Active Session Scan on Startup
    71→
    72→**Decision**: On server startup, Brain queries SurrealDB for `agent_session` records with `session_type = "sandbox_agent" AND orchestrator_status IN ["running", "idle"]` and calls `adapter.resumeSession()` for each. Restoration is fire-and-forget per session with individual error handling.
    73→
    74→**Rationale**: The SDK handles the heavy lifting (event replay, session rebinding). Brain only needs to enumerate active sessions and trigger restoration. Individual session failures do not block other restorations. Failed sessions are marked `status = "error"` with a restoration failure message.
    75→
    76→**Status**: Provisional
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Technology Stack Summary
    81→
    82→| Component | Technology | License |
    83→|-----------|-----------|---------|
    84→| Agent execution | SandboxAgent SDK (0.x, pin version) | Apache 2.0 |
    85→| Session persistence | SurrealDB (existing) | BSL 1.1 (existing dependency) |
    86→| Event streaming | SSE Registry (existing) | N/A (internal) |
    87→| LLM proxy | Brain proxy module (existing) | N/A (internal) |
    88→| Tool governance | Brain dynamic MCP endpoint (existing + new route) | N/A (internal) |
    89→
    90→## Constraints Established
    91→
    92→| Constraint | Enforcement |
    93→|-----------|-------------|
    94→| Orchestrator modules must not import SandboxAgent SDK directly | dependency-cruiser rule |
    95→| No module-level `let` in orchestrator/ | ESLint custom rule |
    96→| All session state must be in SurrealDB, not in-memory | Code review + test coverage |
    97→| Event bridge must not crash on unknown event types | Unit test with fuzzy event input |
    98→| Permission timeout is 60 seconds (configurable) | Config constant with test coverage |
    99→
   100→## Upstream Changes to DISCUSS Assumptions
   101→
   102→| DISCUSS Decision | Change | Reason |
   103→|-----------------|--------|--------|
   104→| D-04 (SurrealDB Persistence Driver) | Confirmed, detailed with 100ms write buffer and per-session buffering | NFR: 50 events/second throughput |
   105→| D-07 (Adapter Interface) | Confirmed, scoped to SDK level (not session level) per Q-04 answer | Testability + single boundary point |
   106→| D-08 (Event Bridge Replacement) | Confirmed, with explicit forward-compatibility for unknown event types | SDK 0.x stability concern |
   107→| None | New: 5 sandbox fields added to `agent_session` (no separate table) | Reuse existing fields, avoid join overhead |
   108→| None | New: server startup restoration scan | Addresses US-05 server restart scenario |
   109→

1→# ADR-074: SandboxAgent SDK Adapter Interface
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→
     5→Proposed
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→Brain's orchestrator needs to integrate SandboxAgent SDK (0.x, Apache 2.0, by Rivet) to replace the Claude Agent SDK `query()` call for sandbox-executed coding agents. The SDK is pre-1.0 with expected breaking changes. Brain follows a functional paradigm with injectable dependencies and no module-level mutable singletons.
    10→
    11→The orchestrator currently imports `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` directly in `spawn-agent.ts` and `agent-options.ts`. A new SDK integration needs a testability seam that allows mocking in acceptance tests and isolates the orchestrator from SDK API changes.
    12→
    13→Quality attributes driving this decision: **maintainability** (isolate SDK churn), **testability** (mock without running SandboxAgent Server), **reliability** (contain breaking change blast radius).
    14→
    15→## Decision
    16→
    17→Wrap SandboxAgent SDK behind a Brain-owned adapter interface defined as TypeScript types. The adapter abstracts at the **SDK level** (one factory creates the adapter, which creates sessions) rather than at the session level. Only the methods Brain actually calls are exposed.
    18→
    19→The adapter port type is defined in the orchestrator module. The production implementation wrapping the real SDK is in a separate module. Tests inject mock implementations directly.
    20→
    21→## Alternatives Considered
    22→
    23→### 1. Direct SDK Usage (No Adapter)
    24→
    25→Import SandboxAgent SDK directly throughout the orchestrator, same as the current Claude Agent SDK pattern.
    26→
    27→- **Pro**: Simpler, fewer files, no abstraction overhead
    28→- **Con**: Every SDK breaking change cascades through orchestrator; tests require running SandboxAgent Server or complex module mocking
    29→- **Rejected**: SDK is 0.x from a startup. Direct coupling is acceptable for stable 1.x+ SDKs but not here.
    30→
    31→### 2. Full Abstraction Layer (Provider Interface)
    32→
    33→Abstract not just SandboxAgent but any agent execution backend (direct process spawn, custom WebSocket, etc.) behind a generic "agent provider" interface.
    34→
    35→- **Pro**: Maximum flexibility for future provider changes
    36→- **Con**: Over-engineering -- Brain has one execution backend (SandboxAgent). Generic interface requires speculative design for unknown future providers. YAGNI.
    37→- **Rejected**: Violates simplest-solution-first principle. Can evolve the narrow adapter into a generic provider if a second backend emerges.
    38→
    39→### 3. Session-Level Abstraction
    40→
    41→Define the adapter interface per-session rather than per-SDK-instance. Each session would be a fully abstract object with no awareness of SandboxAgent.
    42→
    43→- **Pro**: Completely hides SDK internals per session
    44→- **Con**: Spreads the SDK boundary across every session creation site. Event types, permission response types, and prompt message types would all need wrapping. More surface area for bugs.
    45→- **Rejected**: SDK-level abstraction provides a single entry point while still returning Brain-owned session handle types.
    46→
    47→## Consequences
    48→
    49→### Positive
    50→
    51→- SDK breaking changes are contained to one adapter implementation file
    52→- Acceptance tests inject mock adapters without running SandboxAgent Server
    53→- Orchestrator depends only on Brain-owned types, not SDK exports
    54→- Consistent with existing project pattern (ports as types, production implementations injected)
    55→
    56→### Negative
    57→
    58→- One additional level of indirection for SDK calls
    59→- Adapter implementation must be updated when new SDK methods are needed
    60→- Type definitions must be kept in sync with SDK capabilities
    61→

1→# ADR-075: SurrealDB Session Store Over In-Memory Handle Registry
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→
     5→Proposed
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→The orchestrator currently stores active agent session handles in a module-level mutable `Map<string, AgentHandle>` (`handleRegistry` in `session-lifecycle.ts`). This approach has three problems:
    10→
    11→1. **Violates project convention**: AGENTS.md explicitly prohibits module-level mutable singletons ("Module-level state is shared across the entire process -- when multiple server instances run concurrently, they silently corrupt each other").
    12→
    13→2. **Session loss on restart**: All active sessions are lost when Brain's server restarts. The in-memory Map is the only record of running agent processes. This directly blocks the session restoration requirement (> 95% restoration success rate).
    14→
    15→3. **Incompatible with SandboxAgent model**: SandboxAgent SDK communicates with SandboxAgent Server via HTTP API. Session operations (prompt, abort) are HTTP calls to the server, not method calls on an in-process handle. There is no in-process handle to store.
    16→
    17→Quality attributes: **reliability** (session survival across restarts), **maintainability** (eliminate mutable singleton), **testability** (SurrealDB state is inspectable in tests).
    18→
    19→## Decision
    20→
    21→Eliminate the in-memory `handleRegistry`. Store all session state in SurrealDB (`sandbox_session` table). Session operations (prompt, resume, destroy) delegate to the SandboxAgent adapter, which communicates with SandboxAgent Server via HTTP. No in-process session handle is held.
    22→
    23→On server startup, Brain scans for active `sandbox_session` records and calls `adapter.resumeSession()` for each, re-establishing the event stream connections.
    24→
    25→## Alternatives Considered
    26→
    27→### 1. Keep In-Memory Registry + Periodic Checkpoint to SurrealDB
    28→
    29→Maintain the `handleRegistry` for fast access, periodically sync state to SurrealDB for recovery.
    30→
    31→- **Pro**: Low latency for session lookups
    32→- **Con**: Dual-state (memory + DB) creates consistency bugs. Still loses events between checkpoints. Still violates mutable singleton convention.
    33→- **Rejected**: SurrealDB lookup latency (< 5ms local) is negligible compared to HTTP round-trip to SandboxAgent Server (50-200ms).
    34→
    35→### 2. Redis/External Cache for Session State
    36→
    37→Use Redis or similar for session state with SurrealDB as backing store.
    38→
    39→- **Pro**: Sub-millisecond lookups, built-in TTL
    40→- **Con**: New infrastructure dependency. SurrealDB already provides adequate performance. Brain's architecture principle is to keep the graph as the single source of truth.
    41→- **Rejected**: Unnecessary complexity. No evidence that SurrealDB session queries are a bottleneck.
    42→
    43→### 3. In-Memory Map with Dependency Injection (Fix Convention Violation Only)
    44→
    45→Inject the Map via the dependency chain instead of module-level declaration. Still in-memory, but no longer a singleton.
    46→
    47→- **Pro**: Fixes the singleton convention violation
    48→- **Con**: Sessions still lost on restart. Doesn't address the core reliability requirement.
    49→- **Rejected**: Addresses convention but not the business requirement.
    50→
    51→## Consequences
    52→
    53→### Positive
    54→
    55→- Sessions survive server restarts (restoration via SurrealDB scan + adapter.resumeSession)
    56→- No module-level mutable state in orchestrator
    57→- Session state is inspectable in SurrealDB for debugging and auditing
    58→- Single source of truth (no dual-state consistency issues)
    59→- Aligns with existing project patterns (SurrealDB for all persistent state)
    60→
    61→### Negative
    62→
    63→- Every session operation requires a SurrealDB query (small latency addition)
    64→- Server startup has a restoration phase that may take up to 10 seconds for many active sessions
    65→- SurrealDB outage blocks session operations (mitigated by existing SurrealDB reliability practices)
    66→

1→# ADR-076: Sandbox Fields on agent_session (Not a Separate Table)
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→
     5→Proposed
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→SandboxAgent integration requires tracking sandbox-specific state: provider type, replay configuration, event sequence numbers, and session type discriminator. The question is whether this state belongs on the existing `agent_session` table or a new `sandbox_session` table.
    10→
    11→## Decision
    12→
    13→Add sandbox-specific fields directly to `agent_session`. No separate table.
    14→
    15→## Rationale
    16→
    17→Analysis of the existing `agent_session` schema shows most SandboxAgent concepts already map to existing fields:
    18→
    19→- `external_session_id` = sandbox runtime session ID
    20→- `agent` = agent type ("claude", "codex")
    21→- `orchestrator_status` = session lifecycle status
    22→- `worktree_path` = working directory
    23→- `worktree_branch` = git branch for local provider
    24→- `ended_at` = session destruction time
    25→- `last_event_at` = last activity timestamp
    26→
    27→Only 5 new fields are needed: `provider`, `session_type`, `replay_max_events`, `replay_max_chars`, `last_event_seq`. All are optional — Brain-native agent sessions simply don't set them.
    28→
    29→A separate table would have required joins on every session operation, transaction coordination on creation, and a second table to maintain — all for fields that naturally belong on the session record.
    30→
    31→## Alternatives Considered
    32→
    33→### Separate sandbox_session table with record<agent_session> reference
    34→
    35→- **Pro**: Schema separation between agent types
    36→- **Con**: Joins on every session query. Two records to create per session. 10+ fields that duplicate what `agent_session` already has (workspace, status, cwd, timestamps).
    37→- **Rejected**: Over-engineering. The "schema pollution" argument doesn't hold — 5 optional fields on a SCHEMAFULL table have no storage cost when unset, and the project doesn't maintain backwards compatibility with existing data.
    38→
    39→## Consequences
    40→
    41→### Positive
    42→
    43→- Single-table queries for all session operations
    44→- No join overhead on the hot path
    45→- Reuse of existing fields, indexes, and relations
    46→- Simpler session creation (one record, not two in a transaction)
    47→
    48→### Negative
    49→
    50→- `agent_session` gains 5 optional fields unused by Brain-native agents
    51→- Session type branching in queries (`WHERE session_type = "sandbox_agent"`)
    52→

1→# ADR-077: Buffered Event Persistence with 100ms Write Window
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→
     5→Deferred — not needed until cloud provider support (E2B, Docker). For local provider, the SDK's built-in `InMemorySessionPersistDriver` is sufficient since Brain and agent processes share the same host lifecycle. Tracked in [#187](https://github.com/marcus-sa/brain/issues/187).
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→SandboxAgent can emit events at high frequency -- up to 50 events/second during test output or rapid file operations. The `SessionPersistDriver.insertEvent()` method is called for every event. Individual SurrealDB inserts at 50/second would create excessive load and risk backpressure on the event stream.
    10→
    11→The NFR threshold is 50 events/second without backpressure. The event delivery SLA is < 500ms from emission to SSE delivery.
    12→
    13→## Decision
    14→
    15→Buffer events in a per-session in-memory queue. A flush timer fires every 100ms, batch-inserting accumulated events into SurrealDB. During transient SurrealDB outages (< 30 seconds), events continue buffering. On reconnection, the buffer is flushed. Buffer overflow (> 1000 events per session) logs a warning and drops oldest events.
    16→
    17→## Alternatives Considered
    18→
    19→### 1. Synchronous Per-Event Insert
    20→
    21→Call `CREATE sandbox_event` for every event as it arrives.
    22→
    23→- **Pro**: Simplest implementation, zero event loss risk
    24→- **Con**: 50 SurrealDB round-trips/second at peak. Creates backpressure that blocks event delivery to SSE stream. Violates the 50 events/second throughput NFR.
    25→- **Rejected**: Does not meet throughput requirement.
    26→
    27→### 2. 1-Second Batch Window
    28→
    29→Same buffering approach but with a 1-second flush interval.
    30→
    31→- **Pro**: Fewer DB round-trips (10x less than 100ms window)
    32→- **Con**: Up to 1 second of events lost on crash. 50 events potentially unrecoverable. Exceeds acceptable event loss window for session restoration.
    33→- **Rejected**: 100ms window balances throughput and durability. Maximum 5 events at risk vs 50.
    34→
    35→### 3. Write-Ahead Log (WAL) to Disk
    36→
    37→Write events to a local file before batching to SurrealDB.
    38→
    39→- **Pro**: Zero event loss even on process crash
    40→- **Con**: Adds filesystem dependency. Doesn't work in cloud sandboxes where Brain process is separate from agent. Over-engineering for the problem scale.
    41→- **Rejected**: The 100ms window limits exposure to ~5 events at risk. Session restoration works with partial event history. WAL complexity not justified.
    42→
    43→## Consequences
    44→
    45→### Positive
    46→
    47→- Handles 50 events/second with ~10 DB round-trips/second (5x reduction)
    48→- No backpressure on event stream delivery
    49→- Transient SurrealDB outages (< 30s) buffered transparently
    50→- Per-session buffers prevent cross-session interference
    51→
    52→### Negative
    53→
    54→- Up to ~5 events at risk on process crash (100ms window at 50 events/second)
    55→- In-memory buffer adds per-session memory usage (~100KB per session at overflow limit)
    56→- Buffer overflow drops oldest events (logged but unrecoverable)
    57→- Slightly more complex than synchronous writes
    58→

1→# Requirements: Sandbox Agent Integration
     2→
     3→## Business Context
     4→
     5→Brain's orchestrator currently uses `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` to spawn Claude Code processes locally. This architecture has critical limitations: single-shot sessions (no follow-up prompts, 409 on POST .../prompt), in-memory state (lost on restart), no real sandbox isolation (git worktrees only), and tight coupling to one agent type. SandboxAgent SDK replaces the execution layer while Brain retains full governance control through its proxy and dynamic MCP endpoints.
     6→
     7→## Personas
     8→
     9→### Rafael Torres -- Senior Developer
    10→- Uses Brain's coding agents daily for implementation tasks
    11→- Expects multi-turn interaction (prompt, review, redirect)
    12→- Wants session resilience -- long coding sessions should survive network glitches
    13→- Cares about observability: wants to see what the agent is doing in real time
    14→
    15→### Carla Mendes -- Workspace Admin
    16→- Configures workspace settings for her engineering team
    17→- Responsible for security posture (sandbox isolation level)
    18→- Manages cost constraints (sandbox provider costs)
    19→- Non-expert in container orchestration but technical enough to configure settings
    20→
    21→### Brain Orchestrator -- Internal System Actor
    22→- Spawns and manages agent sessions on behalf of users
    23→- Resolves tool grants, registers MCP endpoints, manages lifecycle
    24→- Must maintain governance invariants regardless of execution environment
    25→
    26→## Business Rules
    27→
    28→1. **Governance invariant**: Every MCP tool call from a sandbox-executed agent MUST go through Brain's policy evaluation, regardless of sandbox provider. Native tools (file, bash, git) execute locally in the sandbox without governance.
    29→
    30→2. **Session persistence invariant**: Every session event MUST be persisted to SurrealDB before being acknowledged. Session restoration depends on complete event history.
    31→
    32→3. **Credential isolation**: Agent tokens are scoped per session. One session's token cannot access another session's MCP endpoint or proxy context.
    33→
    34→4. **Provider transparency**: The coding agent does not know which sandbox provider it runs in. Provider selection is a workspace-level configuration, not an agent-level concern.
    35→
    36→5. **Worktree fallback**: When sandbox provider is "local", Brain's worktree-manager.ts creates a git worktree for the session. Cloud/container providers handle isolation internally.
    37→
    38→## Non-Functional Requirements
    39→
    40→| Category | Requirement | Threshold |
    41→|----------|-------------|-----------|
    42→| Latency | Session spawn (local provider) | < 5 seconds from request to first prompt delivery |
    43→| Latency | MCP tool call round-trip (dynamic endpoint) | < 500ms for tool calls to Brain's endpoint |
    44→| Latency | Event bridge delay | < 500ms from SandboxAgent event to SSE stream |
    45→| Reliability | Session restoration success rate | > 95% of sessions with persisted events restore successfully |
    46→| Reliability | Event persistence completeness | 100% of events persisted (no silent drops) |
    47→| Throughput | Event write throughput | Handle 50 events/second per session without backpressure |
    48→| Security | Token scope isolation | Zero cross-session token access |
    49→| Observability | Trace completeness | Every MCP tool call has a trace record with policy evaluation result |
    50→
    51→## Domain Glossary
    52→
    53→| Term | Definition |
    54→|------|-----------|
    55→| **Sandbox session** | A coding agent process running inside a SandboxAgent-managed environment with Brain governance |
    56→| **Dynamic MCP endpoint** | A per-agent HTTP endpoint (`/mcp/agent/<name>`) that serves filtered tools and evaluates policy |
    57→| **Effective toolset** | The union of direct grants (`can_use`) and skill-derived tools (`possesses->skill_requires`) |
    58→| **Session persistence driver** | Implementation of SandboxAgent's `SessionPersistDriver` interface using SurrealDB |
    59→| **Event bridge** | Component translating SandboxAgent's universal event schema to Brain's SSE and trace formats |
    60→| **Walking skeleton** | The thinnest end-to-end slice: spawn, prompt, follow-up, persist, conclude |
    61→| **Sandbox provider** | The isolation backend (local, Docker, E2B, Daytona) configured at workspace level |
    62→
    63→## Dependencies
    64→
    65→| Dependency | Status | Impact |
    66→|-----------|--------|--------|
    67→| SandboxAgent SDK (npm package) | Available (0.3.x/0.4.x) | Core dependency -- entire feature depends on it |
    68→| Brain proxy (`/proxy/llm/anthropic`) | Exists | LLM traffic routing -- no changes needed |
    69→| Brain MCP infrastructure | Exists (from #183) | Dynamic MCP endpoint pattern available |
    70→| SurrealDB agent_session table | Exists | Schema extension needed for SandboxAgent session fields |
    71→| SSE registry | Exists | Event bridge adapts to existing SSE infrastructure |
    72→| Git worktree manager | Exists | Retained for local provider; no changes needed |
    73→
    74→## Risks
    75→
    76→| Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
    77→|------|------------|--------|------------|
    78→| SandboxAgent SDK breaking changes (0.x semver) | Medium | High | Pin version; wrap behind adapter interface |
    79→| SurrealDB event write bottleneck under load | Low | Medium | Batch writes; benchmark in acceptance tests |
    80→| Dynamic MCP endpoint latency spikes | Low | Medium | Connection pooling; timeout with retry |
    81→| Claude Code agent incompatibility with SandboxAgent | Low | High | Walking skeleton validates this first |
    82→| Rivet (SandboxAgent maintainer) abandons project | Low | High | Apache 2.0 license; can fork if needed |
    83→

1→# Acceptance Criteria Summary: Sandbox Agent Integration
     2→
     3→## US-01: Spawn Coding Agent via SandboxAgent SDK
     4→- [ ] Sessions created via `sdk.createSession()` instead of Claude Agent SDK `query()`
     5→- [ ] Local provider creates git worktree; Docker/E2B providers skip worktree
     6→- [ ] MCP endpoint and proxy URL configured on every session
     7→- [ ] Session spawns within 5 seconds for local provider
     8→- [ ] Clear error message when SandboxAgent server is unavailable
     9→
    10→## US-02: SurrealDB Session Persistence Driver
    11→- [ ] `SessionPersistDriver` interface fully implemented (5 methods)
    12→- [ ] Session records stored in SurrealDB with correct schema
    13→- [ ] Events persisted in chronological order with full payload
    14→- [ ] Handles 50 events/second without backpressure
    15→- [ ] Buffers events during brief SurrealDB outages (< 30 seconds)
    16→
    17→## US-03: Event Bridge for SandboxAgent Events
    18→- [ ] SandboxAgent tool_call, file_edit, and permission_request events translated correctly
    19→- [ ] Events appear in SSE stream within 500ms of emission
    20→- [ ] Trace records created for tool calls and file edits
    21→- [ ] Permission requests surface in UI with Approve/Reject controls
    22→- [ ] Unknown event types logged and skipped without crashing
    23→
    24→## US-04: Multi-Turn Prompts via session.prompt()
    25→- [ ] POST .../prompt delivers follow-up via session.prompt() (not 409)
    26→- [ ] Agent retains full context from previous prompts
    27→- [ ] Concurrent prompts queued with 202 Accepted
    28→- [ ] Concluded sessions return 404 with helpful message
    29→- [ ] Multi-turn chain works for 3+ prompts
    30→
    31→## US-05: Session Restoration from Persisted Events
    32→- [ ] Sessions auto-restore after network timeout without user action
    33→- [ ] Active sessions restored on server restart within 10 seconds
    34→- [ ] Event replay uses persisted events from SurrealDB persistence driver
    35→- [ ] Replay respects configurable limits (default: 50 events / 12,000 chars)
    36→- [ ] User notified of restoration status (success or failure)
    37→- [ ] > 95% restoration success rate for sessions with persisted events
    38→
    39→## US-06: Dynamic MCP Endpoint per Agent Session
    40→- [ ] Dynamic MCP endpoint registered per session at `/mcp/agent/<name>`
    41→- [ ] `tools/list` returns only tools from the agent's effective toolset
    42→- [ ] `tools/call` evaluates policy graph before forwarding
    43→- [ ] OAuth credentials injected by credential broker for tool calls
    44→- [ ] Unauthorized tool calls rejected with clear error and trace record
    45→- [ ] Endpoint configured via `setMcpConfig()` with agent token auth
    46→
    47→## US-07: Permission Request Handling
    48→- [ ] Permission requests surface in coding session UI within 500ms
    49→- [ ] Users can Approve Once, Approve Always, or Reject
    50→- [ ] In-scope permissions auto-approved without user interruption
    51→- [ ] Permission decisions recorded in trace graph
    52→- [ ] 60-second timeout with auto-reject for unresponsive users
    53→

1→# DISCUSS Wave Decisions: Sandbox Agent Integration
     2→
     3→## D-01: SandboxAgent as Execution Layer Only
     4→
     5→**Decision**: Use SandboxAgent SDK as the sandbox execution layer, NOT as the tool governance layer.
     6→
     7→**Rationale**: SandboxAgent solves sandbox provider abstraction, agent portability, and session management. Brain's governance (tool filtering, policy evaluation, credential brokerage) must remain in Brain's own MCP endpoint layer. SandboxAgent's MCP config is a transport mechanism (pointing agents at Brain's endpoint), not a governance mechanism.
     8→
     9→**Status**: Provisional (from research, pending implementation validation)
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## D-02: Two-Plane Governance Architecture
    14→
    15→**Decision**: Maintain two separate governance planes:
    16→1. LLM traffic plane (proxy) -- spend tracking, credential forwarding
    17→2. Tool governance plane (dynamic MCP endpoint) -- policy evaluation, tool filtering, credential brokerage
    18→
    19→**Rationale**: Having the proxy execute MCP tools would require running a partial agent loop with edge cases on mixed MCP + native tool calls. The dynamic MCP endpoint lets coding agents route tool calls naturally via standard MCP protocol.
    20→
    21→**Status**: Provisional
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## D-03: Local Provider Retains Brain Worktree Manager
    26→
    27→**Decision**: When sandbox provider is "local", Brain's worktree-manager.ts creates a git worktree and passes the path as `cwd` to SandboxAgent. Cloud/container providers handle isolation internally and skip worktree creation.
    28→
    29→**Rationale**: SandboxAgent's local provider offers zero isolation (host process, full filesystem access). Git worktrees remain necessary for file-level isolation in local deployment.
    30→
    31→**Status**: Provisional
    32→
    33→---
    34→
    35→## D-04: SurrealDB Session Persistence Driver
    36→
    37→**Decision**: Implement a custom `SessionPersistDriver` using SurrealDB rather than using built-in Postgres/SQLite drivers.
    38→
    39→**Rationale**: Session data belongs in the knowledge graph alongside agent_session and trace records. This enables graph queries correlating sessions with tasks, decisions, and observations. Built-in drivers would create a separate data silo.
    40→
    41→**Status**: Provisional
    42→
    43→---
    44→
    45→## D-05: Release Slicing Strategy
    46→
    47→**Decision**: Split into 3 releases:
    48→1. Multi-Turn + Persistence (core migration)
    49→2. Dynamic MCP Endpoint + Governance Parity
    50→3. Agent Portability + Provider Configuration
    51→
    52→**Rationale**: Each release delivers independently verifiable value. Release 1 addresses the highest-scoring opportunities (multi-turn: 18.0, restoration: 16.0). Release 2 is required for production security. Release 3 is strategic but not urgent.
    53→
    54→**Status**: Provisional
    55→
    56→---
    57→
    58→## D-06: Brain-Native Agents Unchanged
    59→
    60→**Decision**: Brain-native agents (chat, PM, observer, analytics) remain in-process with AI SDK. SandboxAgent is only for externally-executed agents (coding agents, user-configured custom agents).
    61→
    62→**Rationale**: Native agents use the proxy's full tool loop where all tools are executed by Brain. Sandboxing them would add complexity without benefit -- they already run inside Brain's process with direct graph access.
    63→
    64→**Status**: Provisional
    65→
    66→---
    67→
    68→## D-07: Adapter Interface Around SandboxAgent SDK
    69→
    70→**Decision**: Wrap SandboxAgent SDK behind a Brain-owned adapter interface to mitigate dependency risk on a 0.x SDK.
    71→
    72→**Rationale**: SandboxAgent is 0.3.x/0.4.x from a startup (Rivet). Breaking changes are expected. An adapter interface allows Brain to swap implementations without cascading changes through the orchestrator.
    73→
    74→**Status**: Provisional
    75→
    76→---
    77→
    78→## D-08: Event Bridge Replacement (Not Extension)
    79→
    80→**Decision**: Build a new event bridge for SandboxAgent events. Deprecate the Claude Agent SDK event bridge.
    81→
    82→**Rationale**: SandboxAgent's universal event schema is a superset -- it normalizes events across all agent types. Extending the existing Claude-specific bridge would create a maintenance burden maintaining two format translators.
    83→
    84→**Status**: Provisional
    85→
    86→---
    87→
    88→## Open Questions
    89→
    90→| # | Question | Owner | Deadline |
    91→|---|---------|-------|----------|
    92→| Q-01 | What is the exact SandboxAgent SDK npm package name and stable version? | Developer | Before US-01 |
    93→| Q-02 | Does SandboxAgent SDK handle prompt queuing internally, or does Brain need to implement it? | Developer | Before US-04 |
    94→| Q-03 | What is the maximum event replay size that maintains agent coherence? | Developer | Before US-05 |
    95→| Q-04 | Should the adapter interface (D-07) abstract at the session level or the SDK level? | Architect | Before US-01 |
    96→| Q-05 | How should permission auto-approve scope be represented in the grant model? | Product | Before US-07 |
    97→

1→# Research: SandboxAgent SDK for Brain Agent Execution
     2→
     3→**Date**: 2026-03-25
     4→**Research Question**: Would SandboxAgent SDK be ideal for agent execution in Brain, particularly for governed tool access via MCP config and skills injection via skills config?
     5→**Prior Art**: `docs/research/brain-native-agent-runtime.md` (concluded Brain should run agents natively)
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. What SandboxAgent Is
    10→
    11→SandboxAgent ([sandboxagent.dev](https://sandboxagent.dev)) is an open-source (Apache 2.0) Rust binary + TypeScript SDK by Rivet that provides a universal HTTP/SSE interface for controlling coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Amp, Cursor, Pi) running in isolated sandboxes.
    12→
    13→**Core value proposition**: One HTTP API to control any coding agent, regardless of their proprietary interfaces.
    14→
    15→**Execution model**:
    16→```
    17→Your Backend → SDK (HTTP) → Sandbox Agent Server (Rust) → Agent Process (Claude Code, etc.)
    18→                                  ↕
    19→                           Sandbox (E2B / Docker / Daytona / Vercel)
    20→```
    21→
    22→**Key capabilities**:
    23→- Session lifecycle management (create, prompt, stream events, resume, destroy)
    24→- Universal event schema across all supported agents
    25→- Permission request handling (approve/reject tool use)
    26→- MCP server configuration per session
    27→- Skills configuration per session
    28→- File system, process, and terminal access within sandbox
    29→- Inspector UI for debugging
    30→- OpenTelemetry observability
    31→
    32→Sources: [GitHub](https://github.com/rivet-dev/sandbox-agent), [Docs](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs)
    33→
    34→---
    35→
    36→## 2. MCP Configuration Analysis
    37→
    38→SandboxAgent supports configuring MCP servers that agents can use within sessions.
    39→
    40→### What it provides
    41→
    42→```typescript
    43→// Set MCP config for a working directory
    44→await sdk.setMcpConfig("/workspace", "my-server", {
    45→  type: "local",  // or "remote"
    46→  command: "/path/to/server",
    47→  args: ["--flag"],
    48→  env: { API_KEY: "..." },
    49→  enabled: true,
    50→  timeout: 30000,
    51→});
    52→
    53→// Remote servers support bearer tokens
    54→await sdk.setMcpConfig("/workspace", "remote-server", {
    55→  type: "remote",
    56→  url: "https://api.example.com/mcp",
    57→  transport: "sse",
    58→  headers: { Authorization: "Bearer ${ENV_VAR}" },
    59→});
    60→
    61→// Session inherits MCP config from its working directory
    62→await sdk.createSession({ agent: "claude", cwd: "/workspace" });
    63→```
    64→
    65→### What it does NOT provide
    66→
    67→| Brain Need | SandboxAgent Support |
    68→|-----------|---------------------|
    69→| **Per-tool filtering** (expose only granted tools from an MCP server) | Not supported — entire MCP servers are enabled/disabled, not individual tools |
    70→| **Dynamic tool injection** based on workspace grants or policy | Not supported — MCP config is static per working directory |
    71→| **Credential brokerage** (OAuth token injection, refresh, rotation) | Basic only — static env vars and bearer tokens, no OAuth flow management |
    72→| **Policy evaluation** before tool execution | Not supported — permission handling is binary approve/reject at the agent level |
    73→| **Tool governance audit trail** | Not supported — events stream tool calls but no policy evaluation records |
    74→
    75→**Critical gap**: Brain's tool registry (#178) requires resolving an agent's effective toolset as `can_use ∪ (possesses → skill_requires)` — the union of direct grants and skill-derived tools. SandboxAgent has no concept of tool-level access control. It exposes entire MCP servers or nothing.
    76→
    77→Source: [MCP Config Docs](https://sandboxagent.dev/docs/mcp-config)
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→## 3. Skills Configuration Analysis
    82→
    83→### What it provides
    84→
    85→Skills are `SKILL.md` instruction files that get loaded into agent context. SandboxAgent supports three source types:
    86→
    87→| Source | Format | Example |
    88→|--------|--------|---------|
    89→| `github` | `owner/repo` | `rivet-dev/skills` |
    90→| `local` | Filesystem path | `/workspace/.skills/` |
    91→| `git` | Clone URL | `https://git.example.com/skills.git` |
    92→
    93→```typescript
    94→// Register skill source
    95→await sdk.setSkillsConfig("/workspace", "my-skills", {
    96→  source: { type: "github", owner: "rivet-dev", repo: "skills" },
    97→  skills: ["sandbox-agent"],  // optional: filter to specific skills
    98→  ref: "main",
    99→});
   100→```

docs/adrs/ADR-042-llm-trace-as-graph-entity.md:52:- **Why rejected**: Duplicates infrastructure that already exists. The `trace` table already has workspace scoping, session linking, hierarchical parent traces, flexible input/output, and duration. A separate table means two trace concepts in the schema, duplicate indexes, and queries that need to UNION across both tables to get a complete session timeline. The trace table was designed to be extensible — its type enum exists precisely for adding new trace kinds.

1→# ADR-075: SurrealDB Session Store Over In-Memory Handle Registry
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→
     5→Proposed
     6→
     7→## Context
     8→
     9→The orchestrator currently stores active agent session handles in a module-level mutable `Map<string, AgentHandle>` (`handleRegistry` in `session-lifecycle.ts`). This approach has three problems:
    10→
    11→1. **Violates project convention**: AGENTS.md explicitly prohibits module-level mutable singletons ("Module-level state is shared across the entire process -- when multiple server instances run concurrently, they silently corrupt each other").
    12→
    13→2. **Session loss on restart**: All active sessions are lost when Brain's server restarts. The in-memory Map is the only record of running agent processes. This directly blocks the session restoration requirement (> 95% restoration success rate).
    14→
    15→3. **Incompatible with SandboxAgent model**: SandboxAgent SDK communicates with SandboxAgent Server via HTTP API. Session operations (prompt, abort) are HTTP calls to the server, not method calls on an in-process handle. There is no in-process handle to store.
    16→
    17→Quality attributes: **reliability** (session survival across restarts), **maintainability** (eliminate mutable singleton), **testability** (SurrealDB state is inspectable in tests).
    18→
    19→## Decision
    20→
    21→Eliminate the in-memory `handleRegistry`. Store all session state in SurrealDB (`sandbox_session` table). Session operations (prompt, resume, destroy) delegate to the SandboxAgent adapter, which communicates with SandboxAgent Server via HTTP. No in-process session handle is held.
    22→
    23→On server startup, Brain scans for active `sandbox_session` records and calls `adapter.resumeSession()` for each, re-establishing the event stream connections.
    24→
    25→## Alternatives Considered
    26→
    27→### 1. Keep In-Memory Registry + Periodic Checkpoint to SurrealDB
    28→
    29→Maintain the `handleRegistry` for fast access, periodically sync state to SurrealDB for recovery.
    30→

1→# Data Models: Sandbox Agent Integration
     2→
     3→## 1. Schema Extension to `agent_session`
     4→
     5→SandboxAgent session state lives directly on `agent_session`. Most fields already exist — only sandbox-specific fields are added.
     6→
     7→### Existing fields reused
     8→
     9→| SandboxAgent concept | Existing field | Notes |
    10→|---------------------|---------------|-------|
    11→| Runtime session ID | `external_session_id` | Already optional string. SandboxAgent runtime ID goes here (changes on restoration). |
    12→| Agent type | `agent` | Already required string. "claude", "codex", "opencode", etc. |
    13→| Status | `orchestrator_status` | Already has values like "running", "completed", "error". Extend with "restoring". |
    14→| Working directory | `worktree_path` | Already optional string. Worktree path for local, sandbox path for cloud. |
    15→| Workspace | `workspace` | Already required. |
    16→| Created | `created_at` | Already required. |
    17→| Started | `started_at` | Already required. |
    18→| Ended / destroyed | `ended_at` | Already optional datetime. |
    19→| Last event time | `last_event_at` | Already optional datetime. |
    20→| Error info | `error_message` | Already optional string. |
    21→| Worktree branch | `worktree_branch` | Already optional string. Still used for local provider. |
    22→
    23→### New fields
    24→
    25→```sql
    26→-- schema/migrations/NNNN_sandbox_agent_fields.surql
    27→BEGIN TRANSACTION;
    28→
    29→-- Sandbox provider: "local", "docker", "e2b", "daytona"
    30→-- Needed on restart restoration to determine isolation strategy
    31→-- (local = Brain manages worktree, cloud = sandbox handles isolation)
    32→DEFINE FIELD OVERWRITE provider ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    33→
    34→-- Session type discriminator: "claude_agent_sdk" (current) or "sandbox_agent" (new)
    35→-- Needed to route session operations to the correct adapter on restart restoration
    36→DEFINE FIELD OVERWRITE session_type ON agent_session TYPE option<string>;
    37→
    38→COMMIT TRANSACTION;
    39→```
    40→
    41→### Fields NOT added
    42→
    43→| Considered field | Why excluded |
    44→|-----------------|-------------|
    45→| `mcp_endpoint_url` | Derivable from agent name: `${brainUrl}/mcp/agent/${agentName}` |
    46→| `event_count` | Derivable via query. Not on hot path. |
    47→| `restoration_count` | Observability metric — belongs on traces/spans, not session schema |
    48→| `last_restored_at` | Same — observability, not session state |
    49→| `replay_max_events` | SDK default (50) is sufficient. Override via adapter config, not per-session DB field. |
    50→| `replay_max_chars` | Same — SDK default (12,000) is sufficient. |
    51→| `last_event_seq` | Only needed for SurrealDB persistence driver (deferred). In-memory driver tracks this internally. |
    52→
    53→## 2. Session Event Persistence (Deferred)
    54→
    55→For R1 (local provider), the SDK's built-in `InMemorySessionPersistDriver` handles event storage for session restoration. Brain and the agent process share the same host — if Brain restarts, the local agent process dies too, so persisted events have no consumer.
    56→
    57→A custom SurrealDB `SessionPersistDriver` with a `sandbox_event` table and write buffering (ADR-077) is deferred until cloud provider support where sandboxes outlive Brain restarts. See GitHub issue for tracking.
    58→
    59→## 3. Permission Decision Storage
    60→
    61→Permission decisions are stored as trace records linked to the session, reusing the existing trace infrastructure rather than creating a new table.
    62→
    63→```sql
    64→-- Permission decisions use the existing trace table with specific fields:
    65→-- trace.type = "permission_decision"
    66→-- trace.metadata contains:
    67→--   permission_id: string
    68→--   tool_name: string
    69→--   arguments: string
    70→--   decision: "once" | "always" | "reject" | "timeout"
    71→--   decided_by: "user" | "auto_approve" | "timeout"
    72→--   response_time_ms: int
    73→```
    74→
    75→## 4. Migration from In-Memory Handle Registry
    76→
    77→### Current State
    78→
    79→```typescript
    80→// session-lifecycle.ts -- module-level mutable singleton (anti-pattern per AGENTS.md)

1→## Git Commits
     2→
     3→- Always use `--no-verify` when committing. The pre-commit hook requires `brain init` which is not available in worktree environments.
     4→- Always use `-s` (GPG sign) when committing.
     5→
     6→## Data Value Contract
     7→
     8→- Never persist, publish, or return `null` for domain data values (Surreal records, API payloads, events, UI state).
     9→- Absence must be represented by omitted optional fields only (`field?: Type`), not by `null`.
    10→- If `null` appears in domain data, treat it as a contract violation and fix the producer. Do NOT sanitize/coerce it at consumers.
    11→
    12→## TypeScript Conventions
    13→
    14→- Do NOT use `null`. Use `undefined` via optional properties (`field?: Type`) instead.
    15→- Do NOT create wrapper/helper functions for simple operations. Cast directly with `as`.
    16→- Type result payloads once and avoid repetitive per-field casting.
    17→- Do NOT use module-level mutable singletons (e.g. `let cache` at file scope) for caching or shared state. Module-level state is shared across the entire process — when multiple server instances run concurrently (e.g. smoke tests with `--concurrent`), they silently corrupt each other. Pass shared state via dependency injection or use per-instance caches scoped to the owning object.
    18→
    19→## Internationalization
    20→
    21→- Use the `Intl` API for all locale-sensitive formatting: `Intl.RelativeTimeFormat` for relative time, `Intl.DateTimeFormat` for dates, `Intl.NumberFormat` for numbers.
    22→- Do NOT hand-roll formatting logic (e.g. custom "5m ago" strings). The `Intl` API handles locale, pluralization, and grammar rules automatically.
    23→
    24→## Browser-Facing Route Authentication
    25→
    26→- Browser-facing routes (UI pages, client-side fetches) must resolve identity from the Better Auth session, NOT from `X-Brain-Identity` headers. The header-based pattern is for MCP/CLI clients only.
    27→- Use `deps.auth.api.getSession({ headers: request.headers })` to get the session, then resolve identity via `identity_person` edge: `SELECT VALUE in FROM identity_person WHERE out = $person LIMIT 1`.
    28→- Return 401 if no session or identity is found.
    29→- See `tool-registry/routes.ts:resolveIdentityFromSession()` or `policy/policy-route.ts:resolveIdentityFromSession()` for reference implementations.
    30→
    31→## Agentic Design: No Hardcoded Modes
    32→
    33→- Do NOT introduce hardcoded processing modes (e.g. `"deterministic" | "llm"`) when behavior should be workspace-configurable via data.
    34→- This is an agentic system — capabilities are defined by workspace admins through definitions, not by code branches. If something can be expressed as a definition with configurable logic, it should be.
    35→- Avoid dual-path dispatchers that route between "built-in" and "dynamic" implementations. One path, driven by data. See ADR-038 for the precedent.
    36→
    37→## Failure Handling
    38→
    39→- Do NOT add fallback logic that masks invalid state, malformed payloads, or contract violations.
    40→- Fail fast: throw immediately when required data is missing or does not match the expected shape.
    41→- Prefer explicit hard failures over silent degradation, synthetic defaults, or "best effort" recovery.
    42→- Only introduce fallback behavior when explicitly requested, and document the reason in code comments.
    43→- Never silently ignore errors (e.g. empty `.catch(() => {})`). Always surface them via logging or re-throw.
    44→
    45→## Graph Node Types
    46→
    47→- Read @README.md § "Key Concepts" for graph node types and § "Architecture" for the layered architecture diagram.
    48→
    49→## Server Architecture Overview
    50→
    51→- Entrypoint is `app/server.ts`; it only calls `startServer()` from `app/src/server/runtime/start-server.ts`.
    52→- Runtime bootstrap is split into:
    53→  - `runtime/config.ts` (env parsing/validation)
    54→  - `runtime/dependencies.ts` (Surreal + model clients)
    55→  - `runtime/start-server.ts` (route registration + Bun server startup)
    56→- HTTP cross-cutting concerns live in `app/src/server/http`:
    57→  - `instrumentation.ts` (`withTracing()` — wide-event span per request with business context)
    58→  - `response.ts` (JSON/headers helpers)
    59→  - `parsing.ts` (request/form-data parsing)
    60→  - `errors.ts` + `observability.ts` (error/log primitives)

Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

Base directory for this skill: /Users/marcus/.claude/skills/nw-distill

# NW-DISTILL: Acceptance Test Creation and Business Validation

**Wave**: DISTILL (wave 5 of 6) | **Agent**: Quinn (nw-acceptance-designer)

## Overview

Create E2E acceptance tests from requirements|architecture|infrastructure design using Given-When-Then format. Produces executable specifications bridging business requirements and technical implementation. Infrastructure design from DEVOPS informs test environment setup.

## Interactive Decision Points

### Decision 1: Feature Scope
**Question**: What is the scope of this feature?
**Options**:
1. Core feature -- primary application functionality
2. Extension -- modular add-on or integration
3. Bug fix -- regression tests for a known defect

### Decision 2: Test Framework
**Question**: Which test framework to use?
**Options**:
1. pytest-bdd -- Python BDD framework
2. Cucumber -- Ruby/JS BDD framework
3. SpecFlow -- .NET BDD framework
4. Custom -- user provides details

### Decision 3: Integration Approach
**Question**: How should integration tests connect to services?
**Options**:
1. Real services -- test against actual running services
2. Test containers -- ephemeral containers for dependencies
3. Mocks for external only -- real internal, mocked external services

### Decision 4: Infrastructure Testing
**Question**: Should acceptance tests cover infrastructure concerns?
**Options**:
1. Yes -- include CI/CD validation, deployment smoke tests
2. No -- functional acceptance tests only

## Acceptance Criteria: Port-to-Port Principle

Every AC MUST name the driving port (entry point) through which the behavior is exercised. This enables port-to-port acceptance tests that make TBU (Tested But Unwired) defects structurally impossible.

Each AC includes:
1. **Observable outcome**: what the user/system sees
2. **Driving port**: the entry point that triggers the behavior (service, handler, endpoint, CLI command)

Without the driving port, a crafter can write correct code that is never wired into the system.

**Features**: "When user {action} via {driving_port}, {observable_outcome}"
**Bug fixes**: "When {trigger}, {modified_code_path} produces {correct_outcome} instead of {current_broken_behavior}"

## Prior Wave Consultation

Before beginning DISTILL work, read targeted prior wave artifacts:

1. **DISCOVER** (skip): DISCUSS already synthesized DISCOVER evidence into requirements and acceptance criteria.
2. **DISCUSS** (primary input): Read from `docs/feature/{feature-id}/discuss/`:
   - `acceptance-criteria.md` — primary input for test creation
   - `story-map.md` — drives walking skeleton priority and release slicing
   - `user-stories.md` — story-to-test traceability
   - `wave-decisions.md` — quick check for upstream changes
3. **DESIGN** (structural context): Read from `docs/feature/{feature-id}/design/`:
   - `architecture-design.md` — port boundaries define test scope
   - `component-boundaries.md` — determines which components tests exercise
   - `wave-decisions.md` — check for upstream changes from DISCUSS
4. **DEVOPS** (test environment): Read from `docs/feature/{feature-id}/devops/`:
   - `platform-architecture.md` — test environment setup
   - `ci-cd-pipeline.md` — test execution context
   - `wave-decisions.md` — check for infrastructure constraints affecting tests

DISTILL is the major synthesis point. Its job is to translate all prior wave knowledge into executable acceptance tests. The acceptance criteria from DISCUSS + architecture from DESIGN + infra from DEVOPS are sufficient. Raw DISCOVER artifacts are not needed — they were already synthesized into DISCUSS requirements.

**READING ENFORCEMENT**: You MUST read every file listed in Prior Wave Consultation above using the Read tool before proceeding. After reading, output a confirmation checklist (`✓ {file}` for each read, `⊘ {file} (not found)` for missing). Do NOT skip files that exist — skipping causes acceptance tests disconnected from requirements and architecture.

After reading, check whether any acceptance test assumptions contradict prior wave decisions. Use `wave-decisions.md` files to detect upstream changes. Example: DISCUSS acceptance criteria reference a "notification email" but DESIGN's wave-decisions.md notes email was removed in favor of in-app notifications — tests must reflect the DESIGN decision.

## Document Update (Back-Propagation)

When DISTILL work reveals gaps or contradictions in prior waves:
1. Document findings in `docs/feature/{feature-id}/distill/upstream-issues.md`
2. Reference the original prior-wave document and describe the gap
3. If acceptance criteria from DISCUSS are untestable as written, note the specific criteria and why
4. Resolve with user before writing tests against ambiguous or contradictory requirements

## Rigor Profile Integration

Before dispatching the acceptance designer, read rigor config from `.nwave/des-config.json` (key: `rigor`). If absent, use standard defaults.

- **`agent_model`**: Pass as `model` parameter to Task tool. If `"inherit"`, omit `model` (inherits from session).

## Agent Invocation

@nw-acceptance-designer

Execute \*create-acceptance-tests for {feature-id}.

Context files: see above.

**Configuration:**
- model: rigor.agent_model (omit if "inherit")
- test_type: {Decision 1: core|extension|bugfix}
- test_framework: {Decision 2: specflow|cucumber|pytest-bdd}
- integration_approach: {Decision 3} | infrastructure_testing: {Decision 4}
- interactive: moderate | output_format: gherkin

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All user stories have corresponding acceptance tests
- [ ] Step methods call real production services (no mocks at acceptance level)
- [ ] One-at-a-time implementation strategy established (@skip/@pending tags)
- [ ] Tests exercise driving ports, not internal components (hexagonal boundary)
- [ ] Walking skeleton created first with user-centric scenarios (features only; optional for bugs)
- [ ] Infrastructure test scenarios included (if Decision 4 = Yes)
- [ ] Handoff package ready for nw-software-crafter (DELIVER wave)

## Examples

### Example 1: Core feature acceptance tests
```
/nw-distill payment-webhook --test-framework=pytest-bdd --integration=real-services
```
Quinn creates Given-When-Then acceptance tests from requirements and architecture, establishes walking skeleton first, then milestone features with @skip tags for one-at-a-time implementation.

## Next Wave

**Handoff To**: nw-software-crafter (DELIVER wave)
**Deliverables**: Feature files|step definitions|test-scenarios.md|walking-skeleton.md

## Wave Decisions Summary

Before completing DISTILL, produce `docs/feature/{feature-id}/distill/wave-decisions.md`:

```markdown
# DISTILL Decisions — {feature-id}

## Key Decisions
- [D1] {decision}: {rationale} (see: {source-file})

## Test Coverage Summary
- Total scenarios: {N}
- Walking skeleton scenarios: {N}
- Milestone features: {list}
- Test framework: {framework}
- Integration approach: {approach}

## Constraints Established
- {test boundary constraint}

## Upstream Issues
- {any gaps found in prior wave artifacts}
```

DISTILL is the major synthesis point. DELIVER reads DISTILL output as its authoritative specification — the acceptance tests encode all prior wave decisions into executable form.

## Expected Outputs

```
tests/{test-type-path}/{feature-id}/acceptance/
  walking-skeleton.feature
  milestone-{N}-{description}.feature
  integration-checkpoints.feature
  steps/
    conftest.py
    {domain}_steps.py

docs/feature/{feature-id}/distill/
  test-scenarios.md
  walking-skeleton.md
  acceptance-review.md
  wave-decisions.md
```

Bug fix regression tests:
```
tests/regression/{component-or-module}/
  bug-{ticket-or-description}.feature
  steps/
    conftest.py
    {domain}_steps.py

tests/unit/{component-or-module}/
  test_{module}_bug_{ticket-or-description}.py
```
